analysis:
The strongest heuristics share a common architecture: deterministic Pareto prefilter → compact, k‑proportional shortlist scored by cheap proxies (rectangular box volumes + few-weight scalarizations) → a greedy/CELF lazy-greedy core that uses cached singleton HVs (and small selective pairwise caches) → tight upper‑bound pruning and a small bounded 1‑for‑1 swap cleanup. These designs minimize expensive pg.hypervolume calls by (a) reducing candidate count, (b) reusing computed HVs, and (c) avoiding needless exact evaluations.

Weaker variants tend to recompute HVs excessively (full-set evals per candidate or per-swap), use Python‑level O(N) membership checks in tight loops, repeatedly vstack/allocate arrays, and lack effective upper bounds or HV budgets. Common avoidable overheads: creating pg.hypervolume objects inside inner loops, repeated conversions between lists and numpy arrays, not using boolean masks/sets for membership, and not precomputing rectified diffs or singleton HVs. Designs that approximate HVC with many weight probes tradeoff accuracy for fewer HV calls but must keep the probe budget tuned to avoid heavy precomputation.

Practical tradeoffs seen: larger shortlists + more HV budget can improve solution quality but increase runtime; aggressive pruning and small deterministic shortlists keep runtime predictable with minor quality loss. Deterministic tie‑breaking and fixed RNGs improve reproducibility. Small pairwise caches and CELF timestamps give large HV savings when shortlist is small.
experience: Always prefilter to nondominated points, vectorize box/weight scores, and build an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only when shortlist≤~40). Use CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and box‑based upper bounds, limit total HV calls, maintain selected points in a preallocated array to avoid vstack churn, and use sets/masks for membership. Finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap pass. Consider lightweight scalar HVC approximations if HV calls must be avoided.
current_reflection: Always prefilter to nondominated points, vectorize box/weight scores, and build an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs (and pairwise only when shortlist≤~40). Use CELF lazy greedy with timestamps and box‑based upper bounds, limit total HV calls, maintain selected points in a preallocated array to avoid vstack churn, and use sets/masks for membership. Finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap pass. Consider lightweight scalar HVC approximations if HV calls must be avoided.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.

Design next heuristics by enforcing an O(k) shortlist, always cache singleton HVs, use CELF lazy evaluation with timestamps, apply cheap box-volume upper bounds to prune candidates, enable pairwise caching only for very small shortlists, cap HV calls and swap iterations, and prefer deterministic stable ordering. These focused changes yield most HV-quality gain per expensive HV call.

Prioritize reducing exact HV calls: build a high-precision O(k) shortlist with cheap proxies, cache singleton (and small pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates and box‑bound pruning, then run a tiny deterministic swap pass. Add an explicit HV-call budget with graceful fallback approximations and vectorized nondomination/precomputation to get consistent, fast, and high-quality k‑subsets.
bad_reflections: Design heuristics that minimize exact HV calls: vectorize prefiltering/scoring, build an adaptive O(k) shortlist, use CELF with singleton caches and timestamps, apply box‑volume upper bounds and only small selective pairwise caching, then run a tightly bounded deterministic swap. Tune shortlist size and weight-vector count to trade quality vs HV budget; preallocate buffers and prefer set membership + vector ops to cut Python loop overhead.

Always filter dominated points, rank with a cheap vectorized proxy, and keep an O(k) shortlist. Cache singleton HVs and use CELF/timestamps to avoid repeat HV calls. Limit HV budget and swap iterations, reuse buffers (avoid vstack per-candidate), and only precompute pairwise HVs when shortlist is tiny. Prioritize deterministic tie-breaks and a small, well‑tuned shortlist for consistent high-quality tradeoffs.

Cache singleton HVs, precompute diffs/box volumes, and use sets/preallocated arrays to avoid Python-level copying and membership costs. Use CELF lazy updates with tight upper bounds (box or scalarized) and an explicit HV-call budget. Prefer a small, well-chosen shortlist (O(k)) with moderate weight vectors; do selective pairwise caching only when shortlist ≤ ~40. Tune swap and shortlist sizes as the primary knobs for quality vs cost.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: nondominated, vectorized scoring, O(k) shortlist, singleton-cache, CELF lazy greedy, timestamps, box upper bounds, pairwise cache≤40, HV-budget, preallocate buffers, deterministic swap
- Advice: prefilter nondominated points, vectorize weight/box scores to build an O(k) shortlist, cache singleton HVs, run CELF with timestamps and box‑based upper bounds, cap HV calls and swap iterations, keep selections in preallocated arrays and use sets/masks, finish with a tiny bounded deterministic swap, fall back to scalar HVC approximations if budgeted
- Avoid: unbounded HV/pairwise caching, per-candidate vstack churn, Python-level nested HV recomputations, nondeterministic tie-breaking
- Explanation: these steps minimize expensive HV evaluations, prune cheaply with proxies/boxes, reuse cached deltas, and deliver stable high-quality k-subsets with predictable cost.
